title: The newborn whose jaundice won't quit
blurb: Unconjugated bilirubin climbing toward the danger zone in a well-appearing term newborn — with phototherapy barely holding the line.

patient.complaint: Deep yellow since day 2; total bilirubin keeps rising despite intensive phototherapy.

opening: 5-day-old term girl, exclusively breastfed, vigorous and feeding well. Jaundice appeared on day 2 and has deepened. Total bilirubin is 28 mg/dL — almost entirely unconjugated — and it keeps climbing despite intensive phototherapy. She is now slightly lethargic with a subtle high-pitched cry. Blood type, Coombs, CBC, retic, and smear are all unremarkable. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Term, uncomplicated delivery, Apgars 9/9. Mother O+, baby O+, direct Coombs negative. No bruising or cephalohematoma. Feeding and voiding well. Parents are consanguineous. An older sibling needed exchange transfusion as a neonate and is now on nightly phototherapy at home.
exam: Vitals normal, afebrile. Deeply jaundiced to the soles (cephalocaudal progression complete). Well-hydrated, good tone earlier but now mildly lethargic with an intermittent high-pitched cry. No hepatosplenomegaly. Setting-sun sign not yet present. Neuro otherwise non-focal.

prompts.diagnosis: What is the most likely diagnosis (and which type)?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why is the bilirubin UNCONJUGATED, and why is phenobarbital useless here (unlike in the milder variant)?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the bilirubin-handling step that fails in this disorder.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — UGT1A1 (bilirubin UDP-glucuronosyltransferase). With it absent, unconjugated bilirubin can't be made water-soluble for biliary excretion, so lipophilic UCB accumulates and crosses into the basal ganglia → kernicterus. Type I = no enzyme (no phenobarbital response); type II = reduced enzyme (responds to phenobarbital).
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The bilirubin is UNCONJUGATED — which step normally conjugates it to make it water-soluble?
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: UGT1A1 (Bilirubin (UCB) → Conj. bilirubin). Same enzyme is mildly reduced in Gilbert syndrome and absent in Crigler-Najjar type I.

mcqs.stem: Why is phenobarbital effective in Crigler-Najjar type II and Gilbert syndrome but NOT type I?
choices: Phenobarbital induces residual UGT1A1; type I has essentially no enzyme to induce, while type II/Gilbert retain partial activity
Type I has a transporter defect, not an enzyme defect
Phenobarbital only works on conjugated bilirubin
mcqs.explanation: Phenobarbital up-regulates UGT1A1 transcription. That helps when SOME functional enzyme exists (type II, Gilbert) but does nothing in type I where the enzyme is absent. Type I requires aggressive phototherapy, exchange transfusion, and ultimately liver transplant.

mcqs.stem: Why does unconjugated (not conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia threaten the brain?
choices: Unconjugated bilirubin is lipid-soluble and crosses the blood-brain barrier, depositing in the basal ganglia (kernicterus)
Conjugated bilirubin is the neurotoxic form
Unconjugated bilirubin precipitates in cerebral vessels
Only direct bilirubin crosses the placenta
mcqs.explanation: Unconjugated bilirubin is lipophilic and albumin-bound; when it exceeds albumin binding (or albumin is displaced), free UCB crosses the BBB and is toxic to the basal ganglia and brainstem nuclei → acute bilirubin encephalopathy and chronic kernicterus. Conjugation makes it water-soluble and non-neurotoxic.

mcqs.stem: How does this differ mechanistically from Dubin-Johnson syndrome?
choices: Dubin-Johnson is a defect in MRP2-mediated secretion of CONJUGATED bilirubin into bile (conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, black liver), not a conjugation defect
Dubin-Johnson is also a UGT1A1 defect
mcqs.explanation: Dubin-Johnson = defective MRP2 canalicular transporter → conjugated bilirubin can be made but not excreted → CONJUGATED (direct) hyperbilirubinemia with a characteristic black liver. Crigler-Najjar/Gilbert are upstream, at conjugation, giving UNCONJUGATED hyperbilirubinemia.
